text:
I hereby propose the Mandatory Pocket Act, which would require every article of clothing sold or manufactured to include at least one functional pocket, and stipulate that no garment may contain a decorative fake pocket, the sewn-shut variety that promises storage and delivers only betrayal.

I know how this sounds. We have wars, plagues, and crumbling infrastructure, and here I am legislating trousers. But hear me out, because the case is stronger than it looks.

First, the fake pocket is a small lie we have all agreed to tolerate, and a society that grows comfortable with small lies is softening itself for larger ones. When a manufacturer stitches a flap onto a garment to imply a pocket that does not exist, they are committing a tiny fraud against the consumer, who reasonably assumes that a pocket is for putting things in. Decorative pockets are the architectural equivalent of a door painted on a brick wall. We would not tolerate a faucet that dispenses only the sound of running water, yet we have made peace with pants that dispense only the idea of a pocket. The Mandatory Pocket Act simply asks that things be what they appear to be, which is the bedrock of all honest commerce.

Second, the distribution of pockets in clothing is one of the quiet injustices of modern life, falling overwhelmingly along lines that no one chose. Garments marketed to women are routinely given pockets too shallow to hold a phone, or no pockets at all, forcing the purchase of additional bags to carry the things that other people simply slip into their trousers. To require functional pockets universally is to redistribute a small but constant convenience, and to spare millions of people the daily indignity of holding their belongings in their hands like a raccoon crossing a river.

Third, consider the environmental dividend. A population confident that their clothing can carry their keys, phone, and wallet is a population that buys fewer disposable bags, fewer redundant purses, fewer little zippered pouches that exist solely to compensate for the pockets we were denied. Every functional pocket is a tiny act of carrying capacity built directly into the things we already own, and multiplied across a nation, that is a meaningful reduction in the clutter we manufacture and discard.

The objections are predictable. Designers will protest that pockets ruin the line of a garment, to which I say that the human silhouette has survived the existence of pockets for several centuries and will endure. Libertarians will cry that the state has no business in our seams, and I concede the point is not trivial, but we already regulate flammability, labeling, and lead content in clothing, so the principle that garments must meet basic standards of non-deception is well established. I am merely extending it to the most fundamental promise a pocket can make, which is to be one.

So I commend the Mandatory Pocket Act to you. It is ridiculous, yes, but only in scale, not in spirit. It asks for honesty, equity, and a place to put your hands. A civilization is entitled to no less.
